name: Coffee, brewed (food medium)
basis: per 100 g (≈100 mL) brewed coffee beverage, fresh

components.name: chlorogenic acid (5-caffeoylquinic acid)
components.source: Rune et al. 2023 Curr Res Food Sci 6:100485 doi:10.1016/j.crfs.2023.100485
components.evidence: Mean 0.81 mg/mL across roasts in French-press specialty coffee; the single most abundant acid; decreases with roast degree. Dominant phenolic carbon source.

components.name: quinic acid
components.evidence: Mean 0.68 mg/mL; rises with roast (chlorogenic-acid hydrolysis). Readily catabolised aliphatic acid.

components.name: citric acid
components.evidence: Mean 0.40 mg/mL; decreases with roast degree.

components.name: acetic acid
components.evidence: Mean 0.28 mg/mL; increases with roast (carbohydrate degradation).

components.name: phosphoric acid
components.evidence: Mean 0.27 mg/mL; increases with roast. Also a phosphate/proton source in the brew.

components.name: lactic acid
components.evidence: Mean 0.17 mg/mL; increases with roast.

components.name: formic acid
components.evidence: Mean 0.17 mg/mL; increases with roast.

components.name: malic acid
components.evidence: Mean 0.16 mg/mL; decreases with roast degree.

components.name: glycolic acid
components.evidence: Mean 0.09 mg/mL; increases with roast.

components.name: D-glucose
components.evidence: 0.39 mg/mL in light roast; free sugars collapse to <0.2 mg/mL total in medium/dark roasts. Most preferred carbon source but low.

components.name: D-fructose
components.evidence: 0.53 mg/mL in light roast; largely lost in darker roasts.

components.name: sucrose
components.evidence: Green-bean sucrose (~6-9% dry) is almost entirely degraded during roasting/Maillard; present only in trace amounts in the brew, not quantified separately here.

components.name: caffeine
components.source: USDA FDC / SR NDB 14209 'Coffee, brewed from grounds, prepared with tap water'
components.evidence: 40 mg/100 g. Poorly catabolised by most microbes and growth-inhibitory; a defining non-nutritive metabolite.

components.name: trigonelline
components.source: Konieczka et al. 2020 LWT (S0023643820303339); Wu et al. 2023 (ResearchGate 370031941)
components.evidence: Brewed coffee ~0.3-1.5 mg/mL (range 30-100 mg/100 mL depending on species/roast); ~50 mg/100 mL typical. Thermally decarboxylates to nicotinic acid on roasting.

components.name: potassium
components.evidence: 49 mg/100 g; the dominant mineral / main cation in the brew.

components.name: phosphorus
components.evidence: 3 mg/100 g (in addition to phosphoric-acid phosphate above).

components.name: copper
components.evidence: ~0.002 mg/100 g; trace.

components.name: fluoride
components.evidence: Variable, derived largely from the tap water used to brew; present but not fixed by the food.

components.name: niacin (vitamin B3 / nicotinic acid)
components.evidence: 0.19 mg/100 g; rises during roasting from trigonelline demethylation.

components.name: thiamin (vitamin B1)
components.evidence: 0.014 mg/100 g; trace.

components.name: pantothenic acid (vitamin B5)
components.evidence: ~0.06 mg/100 g; trace.

components.name: total protein / free amino acids
components.evidence: Total protein 0.12 g/100 g; free amino acids are near-trace because most are consumed in Maillard reactions during roasting. The many individual amino acids in the FooDB record reflect green-bean storage protein, not the brew — the brew is severely nitrogen-limited.

remove.name: Cholesterol
remove.reason: Animal sterol; biologically implausible in a plant-derived beverage; FooDB predicted, not measured.

remove.name: Retinol
remove.reason: Preformed (animal-form) vitamin A; USDA reports 0 in brewed coffee; implausible in a plant beverage.

remove.name: Beta-Carotene
remove.reason: USDA reports negligible/zero provitamin A carotenoid in brewed coffee; FooDB predicted.

remove.name: Cyanocobalamin
remove.reason: Vitamin B12 is not synthesised by plants; not present in coffee; FooDB predicted/implausible.

remove.name: Phytomenadione
remove.reason: Vitamin K1; USDA reports 0 in brewed coffee; negligible in the brew.

remove.name: L-Ascorbate
remove.reason: Vitamin C; USDA reports 0 in brewed coffee; heat/roast-labile and absent from the beverage.

remove.name: Starch C12H20O10
remove.reason: No starch is extracted into the brewed beverage (retained in the grounds); USDA carbohydrate ~0.

remove.name: Nickel
remove.reason: Ultratrace, non-nutritive in this matrix; FooDB contaminant-level entry, not a growth nutrient.

remove.name: Co2+
remove.reason: Cobalt ion at ultratrace/contaminant level; not a defined growth nutrient of the brew.

remove.name: Decanoic acid / Dodecanoic acid / Pentadecanoic acid / Octanoic acid / Heptanoate / Hexanoate / alpha-Linolenic acid / (Z,Z)-9,12-octadecadienoic acid / C18:1 n-9
remove.reason: Filtered brewed coffee contains ~0.02 g/100 g total lipid; these green-bean fatty acids are almost entirely retained in the grounds/paper filter and are not meaningful growth substrates in the brew.

keep_uncovered: neochlorogenic acid / cryptochlorogenic acid (3- and 4-caffeoylquinic acid isomers)
feruloylquinic acids (3-,4-,5-FQA) and dicaffeoylquinic acids (3,4-/3,5-/4,5-diCQA) — genuine coffee chlorogenic-acid family
caffeic acid, ferulic acid, p-coumaric acid, cinnamic acid — hydroxycinnamates
guaiacol (2-methoxyphenol), 4-vinylguaiacol, 4-ethylguaiacol, pyrogallol (1,2,3-benzenetriol), catechols — roast-derived volatile phenols
theobromine — minor purine alkaloid (real coffee compound)
sources: USDA FoodData Central / SR Legacy NDB 14209 'Coffee, brewed from grounds, prepared with tap water'
Rune, Giacalone, Steen et al. 2023, Current Research in Food Science 6:100485, doi:10.1016/j.crfs.2023.100485 (individual organic acids & sugars in French-press brewed coffee)
Konieczka et al. 2020, LWT (S0023643820303339) and Wu et al. 2023 (trigonelline in coffee brews)
notes: For a growth medium, brewed coffee is a dilute (~99% water), nitrogen-poor beverage whose dominant carbon/energy substrates are ORGANIC ACIDS — chlorogenic and quinic acids first, then acetic, citric, formic, lactic, malic — with only modest free glucose/fructose that survive roasting (light roasts only). Caffeine (~40 mg/100 mL) and trigonelline (~50 mg/100 mL) are abundant but largely non-nutritive/inhibitory. Potassium is the main mineral. Acid/sugar amounts come from a specialty French-press study and scale with brew strength and roast degree (acids like quinic/acetic rise, chlorogenic/citric/malic fall with roast), hence medium confidence; minerals/vitamins/caffeine are from the standard USDA drip-coffee panel.
